summary: DHH argues against gamers who judge games primarily by hours of gameplay and multiplayer replay value. Using Max Payne as a case study, he criticizes the vocal contingent demanding infinite replay value from every title, even story-driven single-player games. He draws an analogy to film and music, where audiences judge quality over quantity. He concludes that this quantity-obsessed mindset does a disservice to both players and developers pushing single-player genres forward.
key_points: Multiplayer-heavy games like Half-Life and Quake created unrealistic replay value expectations that get unfairly applied to all games
Max Payne represents a case where four years of focused single-player development is dismissed because it offers 'only' 16-18 hours of gameplay
Movies and music are never subjected to the same quantity-over-quality scrutiny that games face
Judging games solely on hours of content is an injustice to developers trying to advance story-driven genres
money_quotes: Blinded by the dream of infinite replay value in every game, you hear these spoiled gamers proclaim their dedication to only invest their money in games with extensive multiplayer support, whether it fits the style of the game or not.
Since when was movies or music ever subjected to this same level of quantity scrutiny?

Judging games solely on the raw number of hours they're likely to offer is a great injustice to both yourself and the game makers that are looking to further story-driven and other primarily single-player focussed genres.
I don't ever recall anyone putting The Terminator down for only running 107 minutes just because The Matrix goes on for 136.
But I can only show you the door. You're the one that has to walk through it.
key_insight: Games, like films and music, should be judged on the quality of the experience they deliver, not on the raw quantity of hours they provide.
